title: Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2025: Peripheral Immune Tolerance
focus: Discoveries related to central and peripheral immune tolerance, regulatory T cells, and the FOXP3 gene.
rationale: This is a distinct, highly examinable science and technology topic focusing on immunology, specifically the role of T cells and genetic mutations leading to autoimmune diseases.
sectionExcerpt: Mary E. Brunkow, Fred Ramsdell and Shimon Sakaguchi have been awarded Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2025 for their discoveries concerning peripheral immune tolerance. The immune system uses two types of tolerance to achieve this — central tolerance and peripheral tolerance. Central Tolerance happens in the thymus, a small gland in the lymphatic system that produces and trains T cells. Peripheral Tolerance uses Regulatory T cells (Treg cells) to identify self-reactive T cells and stop them from attacking our own tissues. Mary Brunkow and Fred Ramsdell discovered the FOXP3 gene, which controls the development and function of regulatory T cells. If there is a mutation in the FOXP3 gene, regulatory T cells do not form properly, causing a rare autoimmune disease called IPEX.
evidenceBullets: The 2025 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded for discoveries concerning peripheral immune tolerance.
Central tolerance occurs in the thymus, which produces and trains T cells and removes self-reactive ones.
Peripheral tolerance relies on Regulatory T cells (Treg cells) to stop escaped self-reactive T cells from attacking body tissues.
The FOXP3 gene controls the development and function of regulatory T cells.
A mutation in the FOXP3 gene causes IPEX, a rare autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks the body's own tissues.

title: Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) and Superbugs
focus: Definition, mechanisms of spread, and key anthropogenic drivers of Antimicrobial Resistance.
rationale: AMR is a recurring UPSC theme. This section details the biological mechanisms (vertical and horizontal gene transfer) and the specific anthropogenic drivers of AMR across various sectors.
sectionExcerpt: AMR occurs when bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites evolve to withstand the drugs designed to kill them. Strains of bacteria, viruses, parasites, and fungi that are resistant to most antimicrobials are called Superbugs. Spread through vertical gene transfer during bacterial division and through horizontal gene transfer by exchange of resistance genes among microbes. WHO recognized AMR as one of the top ten global health threats. It is often described as a silent pandemic. Key Drivers of AMR: Misuse and overuse of antibiotics in humans; Agriculture & Allied Sector (Use of antimicrobials in intensive farming and the Livestock sector used as growth-promoters); Pharmaceutical Manufacturing (Industrial waste from the production of Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients); Healthcare Facilities; Poor Waste Management.
evidenceBullets: AMR occurs when pathogens evolve to withstand drugs, creating 'Superbugs' that are resistant to most antimicrobials.
Resistance spreads via vertical gene transfer during bacterial division and horizontal gene transfer through the exchange of resistance genes among microbes.
The WHO recognizes AMR as one of the top ten global health threats, often referring to it as a silent pandemic.
Key drivers of AMR include antibiotic misuse in humans, use of antimicrobials as growth promoters in livestock, and industrial waste from Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs) production.

title: Fixed-Dose Combination (FDC) Drugs and Pharma Regulation in India
focus: Definition and benefits of FDCs, and the statutory/regulatory framework governing pharmaceuticals in India.
rationale: This section combines the scientific concept of FDCs with the governance and regulatory bodies overseeing drugs in India (CDSCO, NPPA, Drugs and Cosmetics Act), making it highly relevant for polity and governance questions.
sectionExcerpt: FDC refer to products containing one or more active ingredients used for a particular indication(s). FDCs are considered new drug (under NDCT Rules, 2019) when it combines two or more drugs already approved for individual use in a fixed ratio. Why FDC drugs are Preferred? Better Therapeutic Outcomes, Improved Patient Compliance (Reduces the pill burden), Reduced Cost of Therapy. Regulation of Pharma Products in India: Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 & Drugs and Cosmetics Rules, 1945 governs import, manufacture, distribution, and sale of drugs and cosmetics in India. Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO) under Directorate General of Health Services, Ministry of Health & Family Welfare is National Regulatory Authority of India. National Pharmaceutical Pricing Authority (NPPA) enforces provisions of Drugs (Prices Control) Order. Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP): Schedule M of Drugs and Cosmetics Rules prescribes Good Manufacturing...
evidenceBullets: FDCs contain one or more active ingredients and are considered a 'new drug' under NDCT Rules, 2019 if they combine already approved drugs in a fixed ratio.
FDCs are preferred because they improve patient compliance by reducing pill burden and can lower therapy costs.
The Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 governs the import, manufacture, distribution, and sale of drugs in India.
CDSCO is the National Regulatory Authority responsible for drug approval and clinical trials, operating under the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare.
State Drug Regulatory Authorities grant licenses and conduct inspections at the state level.
The National Pharmaceutical Pricing Authority (NPPA) enforces the Drugs (Prices Control) Order.
Schedule M of the Drugs and Cosmetics Rules prescribes Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP).
